verses.text: “Now Peter and John went up together into the temple, at the hour of prayer, being the ninth hour.”

verses.text: Everywhere we find these two Apostles in great harmony together.

verses.text: And concerning John, Peter said unto Christ, “And what shall this man do?”

verses.text: Now as for the other miracles, the writer of this book omits them; but he mentions the miracle by which they were all put in commotion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe again that they do not come to them purposely; so clear were they of ambition, so closely did they imitate their Master.

verses.text: Why now did they go up to the temple?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Did they still live as Jews?

verses.text: No, but for expediency (χρησίμως).

verses.text: A miraculous sign again takes place, which both confirms the converts, and draws over the rest; and such, as they were a sign for having wrought.

verses.text: The disease was in the nature of the man, and baffled the art of medicine.

verses.text: He had been forty years lame (ch. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

verses.text: And the most obstinate diseases are those which are born with men.

verses.text: It was a great calamity, insomuch that even to provide for himself his necessary sustenance was impossible for him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The man was conspicuous both from the place, and from his malady.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear how the matter is related.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And a certain man, lame from his mother’s womb, was carried, whom they laid daily at the gate of the temple which is called Beautiful, to ask alms of them that entered into the temple.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He sought to receive alms, and he did not know who the men were.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Who seeing Peter and John about to go into the temple, asked an alms.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Peter, fastening his eyes upon him, with John, said, Look on us.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet, not even so were the man’s thoughts elevated, but he persisted in his importunity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For such is poverty; upon a refusal, it compels people still to persist.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let this put us to shame who fall back in our prayers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe, I pray you, Peter’s gentleness: for he said, “Look on us.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So truly did their very bearing, of itself, betoken their character.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he gave heed unto them, expecting to receive something of them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then Peter said, Silver and gold have I none; but such as I have I give thee.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He did not say, I give thee something much better than silver or gold: but what?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he took him by the right hand, and lifted him up.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such was also the way of Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Often He healed by word, often by an act, often also He stretched forth the hand, where men were somewhat weak in faith, that the cure might not appear to be spontaneous.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he took him by the right hand, and lifted him up.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This act made manifest the Resurrection, for it was an image of the Resurrection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And immediately his feet and ankle bones received strength.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he leaping up stood, and walked.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perhaps it was by way of trying himself that he put it thus to further proof, whether perchance the thing done might not be to no purpose.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: His feet were weak; it was not that he had lost them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Some say that he did not even know how to walk.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And entered with them into the temple.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of a truth it was marvellous.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Apostles do not urge him; but of his own accord he follows, by the act of following pointing out his benefactors.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And leaping and praising God;” not admiring them, but God that wrought by them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The man was grateful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You observe how they continued in prayer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The ninth hour:” there they prayed together. [“And a certain man,” etc.] The man was in the act of being carried at that instant. [“Whom they laid daily:”] (his bearers carried him away:) [“at the gate,” etc.] just when people went into the temple.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that you may not suppose that they carried him for some other purpose, but that it was in order that he might receive alms, hear what the writer says: “so that he might receive alms of those entering into the temple.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation of vv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this is the reason why he also makes mention of the places, to give evidence of what he relates.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And how was it,” you may ask, “that they did not present him to Christ?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perhaps they were certain unbelieving men, that haunted the temple, as in fact neither did they present him to the Apostles, when they saw them entering, after having done such great miracles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He asked,” it is written, “to receive an alms.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Their bearing marked them as certain devout and righteous men. [“And Peter fastening his eyes upon him, with John, said,” etc.] (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe how John is everywhere silent, while Peter makes excuse for him also; “Silver and gold,” he says, “have I none.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not say, I have none here, as we are wont to speak, but absolutely, I have none.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What then?” he might say, “do you take no notice of me, your suppliant?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not so, but of what I have, receive thou.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you remark how unassuming Peter is, how he makes no display even to the object of his beneficence? [“In the name,” etc. “And he took him by the hand,” etc.] (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the mouth and the hand did all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such sort of persons were the Jews; lame, and the right thing being to ask for health, these same ask for money, grovelling on the ground: for this it was that they beset the temple—to get money.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then does Peter?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He did not despise him; he did not look about for some rich subject; he did not say, If the miracle is not done to some great one (εἰς ἐκεἵνον), nothing great is done: he did not look for some honor from him, no, nor heal him in the presence of people; for the man was at the entrance, not where the multitude were, that is, within.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Peter sought no such object; nor upon entering did he proclaim the matter: no, it was by his bearing that he attracted the lame man to ask.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the wonder is, that he believed so readily.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For those who are set free from diseases of long standing, hardly believe their very eyesight.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Once healed, he remains with the Apostles, giving thanks to God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he entered,” it is said, “with them into the temple, walking, and leaping, and praising God.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how restless he is, in the eagerness of his delight, at the same time shutting the mouths of the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Also, that he leaped, was to prevent the suspicion of hypocrisy; for after all, this was beyond the possibility of deception.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if previously he was totally unable to walk, even when hunger pressed hard (and indeed he would not have chosen to share with his bearers the proceeds of his begging, if he had been able to manage for himself), this holds still more in the present case.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how should he have feigned in behalf of those who had given him no alms?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the man was grateful, even after his recovery.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And thus on either side his faith is shown, both by his thankfulness, and by the recent event.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was so well known to all, that “they recognized him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And all the people,” it says, “saw him walking and praising God; and they recognized (ἐπεγίνωσκον) that it was he which sat for alms at the Beautiful gate of the temple.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is well said, “they recognized,” inasmuch as he was one unknown now by reason of what had happened: for we use this term with regard to objects, which we find a difficulty in recognizing. [“And they were filled with wonder and amazement at that which had happened unto him.”]

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Needs must it be believed that the name of Christ remits sins, seeing it produces even such effects as this. (“And as he held Peter and John, all the people came together at the porch that is called Solomon’s, greatly wondering.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From his good feelings and love towards the Apostles, the lame man would not leave them; perhaps he was thanking them openly, and praising them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And all the people,” it is said, “ran together unto them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when Peter saw them, he answered.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again it is he who acts, and addresses the people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And in the former instance, it was the circumstance of the tongues that aroused them to hearing, now it was this miracle; then, he took occasion to speak from their accusations now, from their supposition.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us then consider, in what this address differs from the former, and in what it agrees with that.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The former was held in a house, before any one has come over, and before they themselves have wrought anything; this, when all are wondering, and the healed man is standing by; when none doubt, as in the other case where some said “These men are full of new wine.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At the one, he was surrounded by all the Apostles as he spoke; but at this, he has John alone; for by this time he is bold, and become more energetic.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such is the nature of virtue; once started, it advances, and never stops.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe also how it was divinely ordered, that the miracle should take place in the temple, that others also might wax bold, while the Apostles work not in holes (εἰς καταδύσεις) and corners, and in secret: though not in the interior of the temple either, where the greater number were.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then, I pray you, was it believed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The man himself who was healed proclaimed the benefit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there was no reason why he should lie, nor why he should have joined a different set of people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Either then it was because of the spaciousness of the place, that he there wrought the miracle, or because the spot was retired.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe the event.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They went up for one object, and they accomplished another.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus also did Cornelius: he prayed and fasted * * *.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But hitherto they always call Him, “of Nazareth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth,” said Peter, walk.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in the first instance, the thing required was, that He should be believed in.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not, I pray you, give over at the beginning of the story: and if one has named some particular achievement of virtue, and then has dropped it for awhile, let us begin over again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we get into the right mood (ἐν ἕξει), we shall soon arrive at the end, soon reach the summit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For earnestness, it is said, begets earnestness, and dulness begets dulness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He who has effected some little reformation, thereby receives encouragement to approach greater things, and thence again to go on something more than that; and just as it is with fire, the more wood it lays hold on, the more vehement it becomes, so likewise zeal, the more pious reflections it kindles, the more effectually is it armed against their opposites.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As, for example: There are set in us, like so many thorns, perjury, falsehood hypocrisy, deceit, dishonesty, abusiveness, scoffing, buffoonery, indecency, scurrility; again under another head, covetousness, rapacity, injustice, calumny, insidiousness; again, wicked lust, uncleanness, lewdness, fornication, adultery; again, envy, emulation, anger, wrath, rancor, revenge, blasphemy, and numberless others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we effect a reformation in the first instances, not only in them will the success have been achieved, but through them in the following cases also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For reason has then gained more strength to overthrow those other vices.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For instance, if he, who has frequently sworn, once extirpates that satanic habit, he has not only gained this point, but a habit of piety in other respects will have been brought in.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For no one, I suppose, averse to swearing would easily consent to do any other wicked act; he will feel a reverence for the virtue already acquired.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just as the man who wears a beautiful robe, will blush to roll himself in the mire; so is it also here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From this beginning he will come to learn not to be angry, not to strike, not to insult.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if once he has come right in little matters, the whole affair is done.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Often, however, something of this sort takes place, that a person has once reformed, and then again through carelessness falls back into the old sins but too readily, so that the case becomes irremediable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For instance, we have made it a law to ourselves not to swear; we have got on well, for some three, or even four days; after that being hard put to it, we scattered away the whole of our collected gain; we then fall into indolence and recklessness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Still it is not right to give over; one must set to work zealously again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is said, he that has built up a house, and then sees his building pulled down, will have less spirit for building again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, but for all this, one must not be dispirited, but must once more set to work zealously.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us then lay down daily laws for ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For a time let us begin with the easier.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us retrench all that superfluity of paths, and put a bridle on our tongues; let no one swear by God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here is no outlay, here is no fatigue, here is no cost of time.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is sufficient to will, and all is done.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a matter of habit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I beseech and entreat you, let us contribute thus much of zeal.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tell me, if I had bid you contribute your money, would not each one of you readily cast in according to his ability?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If you saw me in extreme danger, would you not, if it had been possible, have cut off your own flesh to give me?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well, I am in danger now, and in great danger, such indeed that, were I withal confined to a dungeon, or had I received ten thousand stripes, or were a convict in the mines, I could not suffer more.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Reach me then the hand.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Consider how great is the danger, that I should not have been able to reform this which is least: I say “least” in regard to the labor required.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What shall I have to say hereafter, when thus called to account?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Why did you not remonstrate? why did you not enjoin? why did you not lay the law before them? why did you not check the disobedient?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It will not be enough for me to say, that I did admonish.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It will be answered, “You ought to have used more vehement rebuke; since Eli also admonished.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But God forbid I should compare you with Eli’s sons.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Indeed, he did admonish them and say, “Nay, my sons, do not so; evil is the report that I hear of you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (1 Sam. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But subsequently the Scripture saith, that he did not admonish his sons: since he did not admonish them severely, or with threats.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For is it not strange indeed, that in the synagogues of the Jews the laws are in such force, and whatever the teacher enjoins is performed; while here we are thus despised and rejected?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not my own glory that I care for (my glory is your good report), but it is for your salvation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Every day we lift up our voice, and shout in your ears.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But there is none to hear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Still we take no strong measures.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I fear we shall have to give an account at the coming Day of this excessive and unseasonable leniency.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore, with a loud and clear voice, I proclaim to all and testify, that those who are notorious for this transgression, who utter words which come “of the evil one,” (Matt. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 37.) (for such is swearing,) shall not step over the threshold of the Church.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let this present month be the time allowed you for reforming in this matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Talk not to me, “Necessity of business compels me to use oaths, else people do not believe me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To begin with this, retrench those oaths which come merely of habit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I know many will laugh, but it is better to be laughed at now, than wept for hereafter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They will laugh, who are mad.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For who, I ask, in his right mind would laugh at the keeping of the commandment?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But suppose they do; why, it will not be at us, but at Christ, that such men will laugh.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You shudder at the word!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I knew you would.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now if this law were of my making, at me would be the laughing; but if Another be the Lawgiver, the jeering passes over to Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, and Christ was once spit upon, and smitten with the palm, smitten upon the face.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now also He bears with this, and it is no wonder (οὐδὲν ἀπεικὸς)!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this, hell is prepared; for this, the worm that dieth not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold, again I say and testify; let him laugh that will, let him scoff that listeth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hereunto are we set, to be laughed at and mocked, to suffer all things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We are “the offscouring” (1 Cor. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 13) or the world, as blessed Paul says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If any man refuse to conform to this order, that man I, by my word, as with a trumpet’s blast, do prohibit to set foot over the Church’s threshold, be he prince, be he even the crowned head.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Either depose me from this station, or if I am to remain, expose me not to danger.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I cannot bear to ascend this throne, without effecting some great reformation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if this be impossible, it is better to stand below.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing more wretched than a ruler who does his people no good.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do exert yourselves, and attend to this, I entreat you; and let us strive, and of a surety more will come of it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Fast, entreat God (and we will do the same with you) that this pernicious habit may be eradicated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is no great matter, to become teachers to the world; no small honor to have it said everywhere, that really in this city there is not a man that swears.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If this come to pass, you will receive the reward not only of your own good works; indeed what I am to you, this you will become to the world.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Assuredly others also will emulate you; assuredly you will be a candle set upon a candlestick.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And is this, you will say, the whole matter?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No, this is not all, but this is a beginning of other virtues.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He who swears not, will certainly attain unto piety in other respects, whether he will or not, by dint of self-respect and awe.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But you will urge that most men do not keep to it, but fall away.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well, better one man that doeth the will of the Lord, than ten thousand transgressors.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact, hereby is everything subverted, everything turned upside down, I mean, because after the fashion of the Theatre we desire numbers not a select number.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what indeed will a multitude be able to profit?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would you learn that it is the saints, not the numbers, which make the multitude?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lead out to war ten hundred thousand men, and one saint, and let us see who achieves the most?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Joshua the son of Nun went out to war, and alone achieved all; the rest were of no use.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wouldest thou see, beloved, that the great multitude, when it does not the will of God, is no better than a thing of naught?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I wish indeed, and desire, and with pleasure would be torn in pieces, to adorn the Church with a multitude, yea, but a select multitude; yet if this be impossible, that the few should be select, is my desire.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you not see, that it is better to possess one precious stone, than ten thousand farthing pieces?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you not see that it is better to have the eye sound, than to be loaded with flesh, and yet deprived of sight?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you not see that it is better to have one healthy sheep, than ten thousand with the murrain; that fine children, though few, are better than many children diseased withal; that in the Kingdom there will be few, but in hell many?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What have I to do with a multitude? what profit therein?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Rather they are a plague to the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is as if one who had the option of ten healthy persons or ten thousand sick folks, should take to himself the latter in addition to the ten.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The many who do nothing well, will avail us only for punishment hereafter, and disgrace for the time being.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For no one will urge it as a point in our favor that we are many; we shall be blamed for being unprofitable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact, this is what men always tell us, when we say, We are many; “aye, but bad,” they answer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold again: I give warning, and proclaim with a loud voice, let no one think it a laughing matter: I will exclude and prohibit the disobedient; and as long as I sit on this throne, I will give up not one of its rights.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If any one depose me from it, then I am no longer responsible; as long as I am responsible, I cannot disregard them; on account not of my own punishment, but of your salvation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For I do exceedingly long for your salvation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To advance it, I endure pain and vexation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But yield your obedience, that both here and hereafter you may receive a plentiful reward, and that we may in common reap eternal blessings; through the grace and mercy of the only-begotten Son of God; to Whom with the Father and the Holy Ghost be glory, power, and honor, now and ever, world without end.
